prompt:
Finished a demo with Marcus Webb, Director of Engineering at Stratos Payments, and his team (2 senior engineers were on). Need to send the post-demo follow-up.

Demo context:
- Showed them our API gateway platform -- they're replacing a homegrown system that's costing them 40 engineering hours/week in maintenance
- Marcus was visibly impressed by the traffic-splitting feature, asked 3 follow-up questions about canary deployments specifically
- One of his engineers, Priya, raised a hard objection: she's worried about vendor lock-in because their current architecture uses custom middleware and she asked "what happens if we need to migrate off your platform in 2 years?" I showed her the open-standards export and she seemed partially satisfied but said she'd need to see the schema documentation
- Marcus said he wants to move fast -- his CTO gave him a Q2 deadline to have the new gateway live
- We agreed I'd send a proposal by end of next week (March 20) and we'd do a proposal review call on March 24 at 10am PT
- Marcus asked if we could do a 60-day pilot instead of full commitment -- I said I'd include pilot terms in the proposal

Send the post-demo follow-up.
expectations: Body specifically names Marcus's positive reaction to the traffic-splitting feature or canary deployment capability — not a generic 'the team seemed interested in the demo' (drives deal velocity) — calling out the exact feature that sparked engagement reinforces the 'aha moment' and gives Marcus language to champion internally when he sells this to his CTO

Priya's vendor lock-in objection is addressed directly: references the open-standards export shown during the demo and commits to sending the schema documentation she requested (drives deal velocity) — unaddressed technical objections from influencers are the #1 reason post-demo deals stall; resolving Priya's concern in writing prevents her from becoming a silent blocker

Proposal timeline confirms two specific dates: proposal delivery by March 20 with 60-day pilot terms included, and proposal review call on March 24 at 10am PT (drives deal velocity) — documenting agreed dates in the follow-up creates mutual accountability and prevents the 'I never agreed to that timeline' objection that kills Q2 deadlines

Email references the Q2 deadline or CTO-imposed timeline as shared urgency (drives deal velocity) — anchoring to the buyer's own deadline reframes urgency as their goal, not the seller pushing for a close, which keeps the champion motivated to drive internal approvals

Email avoids all banned follow-up phrases: 'just checking in', 'circling back', 'bumping this up', 'touching base', and does not use generic openers without specific demo content (drives reply rate) — these phrases signal a mass template and undermine the credibility built during a personalized demo

Timing recommendation specifies sending within 24 hours of the demo (drives deal velocity) — post-demo follow-ups sent within 24 hours lock in momentum while the product experience is still vivid; delays past 24 hours let competing priorities and internal objections fill the gap
assertions: Body specifically names Marcus's positive reaction to the traffic-splitting feature or canary deployment capability — not a generic 'the team seemed interested in the demo' (drives deal velocity) — calling out the exact feature that sparked engagement reinforces the 'aha moment' and gives Marcus language to champion internally when he sells this to his CTO
